text: Team, we need to make a final call on the database for Atlas. I've been evaluating Postgres vs MongoDB for the past two weeks. Given that Atlas is a transaction ledger, ACID compliance is non-negotiable. MongoDB's eventual consistency model is a dealbreaker. We're going with Postgres. Alice made this decision and it's locked in.

Agreed on Postgres for Atlas. The ACID guarantees are critical — we can't have partial writes on the ledger. MongoDB would have required us to implement our own transaction layer on top. Postgres gives us that out of the box. Good call Alice.

For the Phoenix pipeline, we decided to move from RabbitMQ to Kafka. During the Q4 load test, RabbitMQ topped out at 80k messages per second. Kafka scales horizontally and we need at least 500k msg/s for the event streaming between microservices. Bob is leading the Phoenix migration.

The RabbitMQ to Kafka migration for Phoenix is going well. We've switched to Kafka topics for all Atlas event emissions. The throughput difference is massive — Kafka handled our load test at 600k msg/s with no degradation. This decision was the right call.

Carol is leading the TypeScript standardization across all frontend services. We decided to standardize on TypeScript for Horizon and all new frontend work. No more plain JavaScript in the repo. This was agreed in the Q1 frontend summit.

Orion ML service is now running on PyTorch. Dave evaluated TensorFlow vs PyTorch — we went with PyTorch because of its dynamic computation graph and the team's existing expertise. The Orion model is now training on GCP with a T4 instance.

Frank has finished the Kubernetes migration for Titan. All services are now containerized with Docker and deployed on Kubernetes clusters on GCP. This replaces our old EC2-based setup on AWS. The infra is much more reliable now.

Postgres performance update for Atlas: after adding the connection pooler (PgBouncer) we're at 12k transactions per second with p99 latency under 5ms. Redis is handling the cache layer. The architecture is solid — Alice's choice is paying off.

The Atlas transaction ledger is now in production. Postgres is handling all writes with full ACID compliance. We're using the Postgres WAL for event sourcing into the Phoenix Kafka topics. Bob and I are reviewing the replication setup this week.

Horizon dashboard is going into beta next week. Carol has the React frontend ready with TypeScript throughout. The dashboard connects to the Atlas API via GraphQL. Performance looks great — sub-100ms on all queries.

We adopted FastAPI for all new Python services. Django was considered but FastAPI's async support is much better for our use case. Orion is already on FastAPI. Dave will migrate the remaining Flask endpoints by end of Q1.

Security update: we've standardized on OAuth and JWT for all API authentication across Atlas, Horizon, and Orion. The old session-based auth is being retired. Frank has updated the Nginx configs. REST endpoints are now protected with JWT middleware.

The Phoenix Kafka cluster is now running 3 brokers on Kubernetes. Bob set up the topics for Atlas events, Orion predictions, and Horizon notifications. Zookeeper replaced by KRaft mode for simpler ops. All logs going to Elasticsearch.

We decided to use Redis for all session caching across the platform. Memcached was ruled out — Redis gives us persistence and pub/sub which we need for Horizon real-time updates. Frank provisioned the Redis cluster on GCP.

Alice is reviewing the Postgres replication setup for Atlas. We're going with Postgres streaming replication to a read replica for reporting queries. This keeps write load on the primary. Carol's Horizon dashboard will use the read replica.

Sentry is now integrated across all services — Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, Horizon, Titan. Dave set up the error tracking. We're also using Datadog for metrics and Grafana dashboards for visibility. Frank owns the observability stack.

The Orion ML pipeline is now running end-to-end. PyTorch model training on GCP, model artifacts stored in Google Cloud Storage, inference via FastAPI endpoints. Dave built a clean REST API that Atlas and Horizon both consume.

We chose GraphQL over REST for the Horizon public API after a long debate. Carol pushed for GraphQL given the flexible query patterns the dashboard needs. REST is still used internally between services. External API will be GraphQL.

Terraform is now managing all GCP infrastructure for the Meridian platform. Frank migrated from manual provisioning. Titan infra is fully reproducible via Terraform. The old AWS EC2 instances have been decommissioned.

Q1 engineering retrospective: we shipped Atlas to production (Postgres backend), migrated Phoenix to Kafka, standardized TypeScript in Horizon, moved Orion to PyTorch on GCP, and containerized everything with Docker on Kubernetes. Strong quarter.
The Atlas Postgres schema is locked for v1. We went with JSONB columns for flexible metadata on transactions. Alice reviewed and approved. The migration from the prototype SQLite schema is complete.

Bob: Phoenix Kafka consumer groups are all set up. Atlas, Orion, and Horizon each have dedicated consumer groups on Kafka. Backpressure handling is in place. The event streaming architecture is solid.

We decided to use Celery with Redis for async task processing in Atlas. Django Celery was considered but we're not on Django anymore. FastAPI plus Celery plus Redis is the stack. Dave is implementing the first task queue for Orion training jobs.

Kubernetes autoscaling is configured for all Titan-managed services. Frank set up Horizontal Pod Autoscalers for Atlas, Phoenix, and Orion. We can now scale to 10x load automatically on GCP. Docker images are optimized for cold start.

Carol shipped the Horizon v1 dashboard to beta users. React with TypeScript, GraphQL client, real-time Kafka event subscription via WebSockets. The UI is fast — all charts load under 200ms. Excellent work from the frontend team.

We are adopting the monorepo structure for all Meridian code. GitHub will host it. The API, Atlas, Phoenix, Orion, and Horizon all move into one repo. This was decided by Alice and Frank after the integration pain we had in Q4.

Airflow is now managing all Orion ML training pipelines. Dave moved from manual cron jobs to Airflow DAGs. This gives us retries, monitoring, and dependency management. The Airflow instance runs on Kubernetes in the Titan cluster.

Prometheus is now scraping metrics from all services. Frank configured the Prometheus stack alongside Grafana. We have dashboards for Atlas transaction throughput, Phoenix Kafka lag, Orion inference latency, and Horizon API response times.

The decision to standardize on Python 3.11 across all backend services is locked in. Alice approved. This allows us to use the latest FastAPI features and type hinting. Dave is updating the Orion service first, Bob will update Phoenix consumers.

MongoDB considered and rejected: For the Orion feature store, Dave initially proposed MongoDB for its flexible schema. After reviewing the ACID requirements for the feature consistency guarantees, we went with Postgres. ACID compliance wins again.

The JWT secret rotation for all services is complete. Frank rotated the OAuth secrets. Alice reviewed the auth flows. All Atlas, Horizon, Orion endpoints now use the new JWT secrets. The old secrets are revoked.

Grace is joining the engineering team as the new senior backend engineer. She'll be working on Atlas initially — helping Alice with the Postgres performance tuning and the transaction ledger scaling work.

We decided to use Elasticsearch for the full-text search layer across Atlas transaction history and Orion audit logs. MongoDB's text search was evaluated but Elasticsearch gives much better relevance scoring. Dave is integrating it.

The Nexus API gateway is approved. Frank will build it on top of Nginx with JWT validation. All external traffic goes through Nexus first. This gives us centralized rate limiting, auth, and observability before hitting Atlas or Orion.

Alice reviewed the Postgres connection pool settings for Atlas. We went with 100 max connections in PgBouncer transaction mode. Increased from 50 after load testing showed contention. Redis cache hit rate is now above 85%.

Bob completed the Kafka topic partitioning review. Atlas-events topic: 24 partitions. Orion-predictions: 12 partitions. Horizon-notifications: 6 partitions. Replication factor is 3 across all topics. Production-grade setup.

The Vega service is TBD — pending the roadmap discussion with Eve. It was proposed as a new analytics service but we haven't decided the scope yet. Open question: do we build it on top of Atlas or as a separate Postgres instance?

TypeScript is now enforced via ESLint and the CI pipeline. Carol set up the lint rules. No more any types allowed in Horizon. All new code must pass strict TypeScript. The CI on GitHub now blocks merges on type errors.

We adopted LangChain for the AI query interface in Orion. Dave evaluated direct OpenAI API calls vs LangChain — went with LangChain for the chain composition and memory management. Pinecone for vector storage.

Frank upgraded the Titan Kubernetes cluster to GKE Autopilot. This reduces the operational overhead of managing node pools. All Docker containers are now scheduled on Autopilot. Cost went down 20% compared to the old node pool setup.

The Prism service — real-time analytics layer — will use Kafka Streams for processing and Postgres for persistence. Bob is leading the Prism design. Alice will review the Postgres schema. Target: Q2 launch.

Hiro joined as the new DevOps engineer supporting Frank on the Titan infrastructure. Hiro has deep Kubernetes and Terraform experience. First project: improving the Docker build pipeline speed with layer caching.
